title: PT-FB-009 · What clinical trials actually are
subtitle: Education / Myth-Busting
body:
**Series:** Education / Myth-Busting
**Condition / Topic:** What clinical trials actually are
**Content ID:** PT-FB-009

---

**Post Copy:**

Let's bust a myth.

Clinical trials are not a last resort.

Most people think clinical trials are for people who've "run out of options." But the truth is more interesting:

— Some trials are studying preventive approaches for people who aren't sick yet.
— Some are studying quality of life improvements, not just survival rates.
— Some can be done from home, with no clinic visits at all.
— Many are studying conditions that mainstream medicine hasn't solved yet — not because nothing works, but because researchers are still looking for what works best.

You don't have to be desperate to be curious.

TrialScreen has over 120,000 trials, written in plain language. You can search, read, and decide. No one will follow up unless you want them to.

What myths have you heard about clinical trials? Drop them in the comments — we'll address them. 👇

**Post Copy:**

Here's something the medical community has been grappling with for decades:

Most clinical research was done on a narrow group of people. Mostly white. Mostly male. Mostly Western.

That means treatments and dosages were developed based on data that doesn't always apply to everyone.

Researchers know this is a problem. Many trials today are actively working to include more diverse participants — different ages, different ethnicities, different health backgrounds.

Not because diversity is a box to tick. Because research that reflects the full range of human bodies is better research. For everyone.

If you've ever felt like the healthcare system wasn't built for someone like you — your instinct may have been right.

TrialScreen is building a platform where everyone can find research that's relevant to them.

title: PT-FB-011 · Trial safety and consent
subtitle: Education / Myth-Busting
body:
**Series:** Education / Myth-Busting
**Condition / Topic:** Trial safety and consent
**Content ID:** PT-FB-011

---

**Post Copy:**

"What if something goes wrong?"

It's the question everyone has when they first hear about clinical trials. And it's a completely fair one.

Here's what you should know:

Every clinical trial involving human participants goes through an ethics review — called an Institutional Review Board (IRB) or Ethics Committee — before it can start. Their job is to make sure the trial is safe, the risks are understood, and participants are protected.

Before you join any trial, you go through a process called informed consent. Researchers have to explain everything: what the trial involves, what the risks are, what happens to your data, and — critically — that you can leave at any time.

That last one matters: **you can always withdraw from a trial. No questions asked.**

TrialScreen doesn't make the decision for you. We just help you find and understand the options.

**Suggested Visual:** Clean infographic: "The 3 layers of trial safety" — Ethics review / Informed consent / Right to withdraw. Simple, warm colours.

**Hashtags:** #ClinicalTrialSafety #InformedConsent #HealthLiteracy #TrialScreen #PatientRights

**CTA:** "Learn more about how trials work at TrialScreen.org/resources."

**Compliance Note:** Accuracy review required — all safety statements should be verified for accuracy across jurisdictions (AU, US, EU). No IRB required for education post.

title: PT-FB-014 · Researcher Q&A recap
subtitle: Research Friends Community
body:
**Series:** Research Friends Community
**Condition / Topic:** Researcher Q&A recap
**Content ID:** PT-FB-014

---

**Post Copy:**

Last week, we hosted a live Q&A with Dr. [Researcher Name, TBC], a clinical researcher studying [condition, TBC].

Here are the three questions our community asked most:

**"Do I need to stop my current medication to join a trial?"**
Not necessarily. Many trials specifically recruit people who are already on standard medications. Your eligibility depends on the specific trial. The trial team will go through this with you in detail.

**"Will I get the real treatment or a placebo?"**
It depends on the trial design. Some trials use placebos. Some don't. Some use what's called an active comparator — meaning both groups get a real treatment, just different ones. Researchers are required to tell you this before you consent.

**"What happens to my data?"**
Your data is protected by strict ethics and privacy guidelines. Researchers can only use it for the purpose you agreed to, and it's typically de-identified before analysis.

More questions? Drop them below — Dr. [Name] has agreed to answer a few in the comments.

Sarah was 34 when she was diagnosed with systemic sclerosis.

"I remember the rheumatologist using the word 'scleroderma' and thinking — what does that even mean? He gave me a pamphlet. I went home and Googled it for five hours."

For two years, Sarah managed her symptoms and attended regular appointments. She didn't think research was "for someone like her."

Then she found TrialScreen.

"I typed in scleroderma and there were actual trials. Not vague, not complicated. It told me what they were studying, where, and whether I might be eligible. I cried, honestly, because I felt like someone was working on it."

Sarah spoke to her specialist and ended up joining a trial studying a new approach to managing skin involvement in systemic sclerosis. She's now in month eight.

"I'm not doing it to be heroic," she says. "I'm doing it because it felt like the right decision for me. That's it."

You decide if this is right for you. But if you have scleroderma and want to know what's out there — we can help you look.

title: BA-001
subtitle: Blog Articles
body:
**Headline:** 5 Things That Actually Happen in a Clinical Trial

**URL slug:** /resources/what-happens-in-a-clinical-trial

**Meta description:** Most people have the wrong idea about clinical trials. Here's what actually happens — in plain language, no jargon.

**Author:** TrialScreen Editorial Team | **Date:** [Publication date] | **Read time:** 7 min

**Primary keyword:** what happens in a clinical trial
**Secondary keywords:** clinical trial process, how to join a clinical trial, clinical trial myths

---

**Introduction**

You've heard the term. Maybe you've even been told about one by a doctor, or found one online at 11pm while searching about your condition.

But what actually happens when you join a clinical trial?

Most of the information out there was written by researchers, for other researchers. Which means it's full of terms like "randomised placebo-controlled double-blind study" — and short on plain-language explanations of what that actually means for you, on a Tuesday afternoon.

This article walks through the five key things that actually happen in a clinical trial. Not the science version. The real version.

---

**H2: 1. You go through a screening process — and you might not qualify**

The first thing that happens after you express interest in a trial is a screening process. This is designed to figure out whether you meet the eligibility criteria for that specific study.

Every trial has what's called "inclusion criteria" (things that must be true about you — like your age, diagnosis, or current health status) and "exclusion criteria" (things that rule you out, like certain medications or prior treatments).

Here's something important: **being screened out isn't a rejection.** It means the trial was designed for a very specific type of participant, and you don't happen to match it — this time. That's not a verdict on your health or your situation.

Roughly two-thirds of people who are screened for trials don't ultimately enrol. This is normal. It's why TrialScreen shows you multiple options — so you have more than one place to look.

*H3: What screening involves*

Screening might include: a phone call with the research team, a review of your medical records (with your permission), a blood test or physical examination at a clinic, or an online questionnaire. The trial team will tell you what's required before you commit to anything.

---

**H2: 2. You go through informed consent — and it takes time**

Before you start a trial, you'll be given what's called an Informed Consent Form (ICF). This is a detailed document that explains everything about the trial: what it's testing, what you'll be asked to do, what the potential risks are, how your data will be used, and — critically — that you can leave the trial at any time, for any reason.

The informed consent process isn't a box-ticking exercise. Good research teams take it seriously. You should be given time to read the document, ask questions, and — if you want — take it home and talk to a family member or your GP before signing.

**You are never obligated to sign immediately.**

*H3: Your rights during consent*

Under research ethics laws in most countries, you have the right to:

- Ask as many questions as you like
- Take time before deciding
- Have a support person present
- Withdraw your consent at any time — even after you've started the trial

If a research team rushes you through informed consent, that's a red flag. Good trials don't do this.

---

**H2: 3. You'll be allocated to a group (and you may not know which one)**

Many trials use a design called "randomisation." This means you're randomly assigned to one of two or more groups — often a "treatment group" (who gets the intervention being tested) and a "control group" (who might get a placebo, a standard treatment, or a different dose).

The reason for this is scientific: randomisation removes bias and makes the results more meaningful for everyone. But for participants, it can feel strange — you might not get the new treatment.

*H3: What about placebos?*

Placebos (inactive treatments) are used in some trials, but not all. In trials for serious conditions, it's often unethical to give a placebo — so the "control group" typically receives the current standard-of-care treatment rather than a blank pill.

In "blinded" trials, neither you nor the research team knows which group you're in until the study ends. This is done to prevent unconscious bias in measuring results.

Before you consent, you'll always be told whether the trial uses placebos and how randomisation works.

---

**H2: 4. You'll have regular check-ins — some may be remote**

Once you're enrolled, you'll have a series of follow-up visits or check-ins with the research team. These might involve physical examinations, lab tests, questionnaires, or simply a phone or video call.

The number and frequency of check-ins varies enormously by trial. Some intensive trials require weekly visits for months. Others are more hands-off — a check-in every few months, or even just an annual questionnaire.

Increasingly, trials are using "decentralised" formats where most or all check-ins happen remotely — via telehealth, an app, or samples collected at your local GP rather than a specialist clinic.

*H3: What happens if something changes?*

If you experience a side effect, a change in your health, or a change in your circumstances, you should tell the research team immediately. They're required to monitor your safety throughout the trial. If there's a concern, the trial can be paused, modified, or you can be removed from the study — for your protection.

---

**H2: 5. The trial ends — but you may be followed up**

When the trial period ends, your active participation finishes. But many trials include a "follow-up" phase where the research team continues to check in with you — monthly, annually, or at specific intervals — to track long-term outcomes.

You'll usually be informed of any follow-up requirements before you consent.

*H3: What happens to the results?*

Once the trial is complete, the research team analyses the data and publishes their findings — usually in a medical journal. This process takes months or sometimes years after the trial closes.

Some trials commit to informing participants of the results directly. If this matters to you, ask before you join.

---

**H2: Frequently Asked Questions**

**Q: Can I be in a clinical trial while taking other medications?**
It depends on the trial. Some trials specifically want participants who are on standard medications. Others require a "washout period" — time off current medications before joining. You'll always be told this during screening.

**Q: Will my regular doctor know I'm in a trial?**
In most cases, yes — and it's recommended. Good research practice includes communication with your GP or specialist. You should also inform them yourself.

**Q: What if I want to leave the trial?**
You can leave at any time. You don't owe the research team an explanation. Your right to withdraw is protected by research ethics laws in every country that follows the Declaration of Helsinki.

**Q: Is there any cost to me?**
In most clinical trials, the intervention (medication, device, programme) is provided free of charge. Many trials also reimburse travel costs. You should always ask about any out-of-pocket costs before you consent.

**Q: How do I find trials that are right for me?**
TrialScreen lists over 120,000 active and recruiting trials, written in plain language. You can search by condition, location, and even whether the trial is home-based. Start at [TrialScreen.org](https://trialscreen.org).

---

**Related articles:**
- "How to Talk to Your Doctor About Clinical Trials"
- "Decentralised Clinical Trials: What It Means to Participate from Home"
- "Scleroderma Clinical Trials: A Complete Guide"

**CTA:** Ready to see what's being studied for your condition? Search 120,000+ trials in plain language at [TrialScreen.org](https://trialscreen.org). Free, no sign-up required.

title: BA-002
subtitle: Blog Articles
body:
**Headline:** Scleroderma Clinical Trials: A Complete Guide for Patients and Families

**URL slug:** /resources/scleroderma-clinical-trials-guide

**Meta description:** Living with scleroderma or systemic sclerosis? This plain-language guide covers what's being studied, how to search for trials, and what participation looks like.

**Author:** TrialScreen Editorial Team, reviewed by Scleroderma Australia | **Date:** [Publication date] | **Read time:** 12 min

**Primary keyword:** scleroderma clinical trials
**Secondary keywords:** systemic sclerosis research, scleroderma treatment trials, rare disease clinical trials Australia

---

**Introduction**

Scleroderma is a diagnosis that often arrives with more questions than answers.

Most people have never heard of it before their own diagnosis. The name sounds alarming. The information online ranges from outdated to overwhelming. And your specialist — as knowledgeable as they are — may be one of only a handful in your region who sees scleroderma regularly.

This guide is designed to answer one specific question: what is actually being studied for scleroderma right now, and how do you find research that might be relevant to you?

It's written in plain language, because that's what you deserve.

---

**H2: What is scleroderma?**

Scleroderma — also called systemic sclerosis — is a chronic autoimmune condition in which the immune system triggers excessive production of collagen. Collagen is a protein that normally forms the structure of skin and connective tissue. When there's too much of it, tissue hardens and thickens.

In scleroderma, this process can affect the skin, joints, blood vessels, and — in more severe forms — internal organs including the lungs, heart, kidneys, and gastrointestinal tract.

*H3: Limited vs. Diffuse Scleroderma*

There are two main subtypes:

**Limited cutaneous scleroderma** (previously called CREST syndrome) primarily involves skin changes on the hands, face, feet, and lower arms. Organ involvement is less common, though pulmonary hypertension can develop over time.

**Diffuse cutaneous scleroderma** involves more widespread skin thickening and carries a higher risk of internal organ involvement, particularly in the lungs and kidneys.

*H3: How common is it?*

Scleroderma is considered a rare disease. Estimates vary, but approximately 2.5 million people worldwide live with some form of the condition. It affects women more often than men, and onset typically occurs between the ages of 30 and 50. Scleroderma Australia provides support and resources for Australians living with this diagnosis.

---

**H2: What are researchers studying?**

Research into scleroderma covers several active areas. Here's a plain-language overview of where the science is right now.

*H3: Skin fibrosis*

One of the most visible and distressing aspects of scleroderma is the hardening and tightening of the skin. Researchers are studying a range of medications — including some already used in other autoimmune conditions — to see whether they could slow or reduce skin fibrosis.

*H3: Lung involvement (ILD and pulmonary hypertension)*

Interstitial lung disease (ILD) and pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH) are serious complications of scleroderma and are an active area of research. Several trials are studying whether existing treatments, new biologics, or combinations of medications could reduce lung inflammation or slow disease progression.

*H3: Raynaud's phenomenon*

Nearly all people with scleroderma experience Raynaud's phenomenon — extreme sensitivity to cold causing the fingers and toes to change colour and become painful. Researchers are testing various approaches to managing this, from medications to lifestyle interventions.

*H3: Fatigue and quality of life*

Some of the most important research in scleroderma doesn't focus on reversing the disease — it focuses on helping people live better with it. Fatigue, pain, mental health impacts, and daily function are active research areas.

*H3: Stem cell transplantation*

For carefully selected patients with severe diffuse scleroderma, haematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HSCT) has shown promise in some research. This is a high-intensity intervention studied in specialist centres. Eligibility is narrow and the evidence base is still developing.

---

**H2: How clinical trials for scleroderma work**

Clinical trials for scleroderma follow the same basic structure as trials for any other condition — but there are some things worth knowing about how rare disease trials specifically work.

*H3: Smaller participant numbers*

Because scleroderma is a rare disease, trials often have fewer participants than mainstream studies. This is sometimes seen as a limitation, but it also means each participant's contribution to the evidence base is significant.

*H3: International collaboration*

Many scleroderma trials are international, recruiting participants across multiple countries simultaneously. This is because no single country has enough patients to make a trial feasible on its own. For Australian patients, this means some trials open here are part of larger global studies.

*H3: Phase considerations*

Scleroderma trials include Phase 1 studies (early safety testing, small groups), Phase 2 studies (testing whether the treatment appears to do what researchers hope), and Phase 3 studies (larger comparisons against standard care). The phase matters: earlier-phase trials carry more unknowns, but may give access to interventions years before they're commercially available.

---

**H2: What does participation look like?**

Participation varies significantly by trial. Here's what you might expect in a typical scleroderma trial:

- **Screening visit:** A detailed review of your medical history, current medications, and symptom profile. May involve blood tests, skin assessments, and lung function testing.
- **Baseline visit:** Establishing your starting point — measurements and tests that will be compared over the course of the trial.
- **Treatment phase:** Regular clinic visits (frequency varies) for monitoring, administration of study medication, or assessments.
- **Follow-up phase:** After the active treatment period, ongoing check-ins to track outcomes.

Some newer trials have decentralised components — phone or video check-ins, home-based symptom tracking, or blood samples taken at a local clinic rather than a specialist centre.

---

**H2: How to search for scleroderma trials on TrialScreen**

TrialScreen lists scleroderma and systemic sclerosis trials from multiple registries, rewritten in plain language.

To search:
1. Go to [TrialScreen.org](https://trialscreen.org)
2. Type "scleroderma" or "systemic sclerosis" in the search bar
3. Use the location filter to see what's available near you
4. Read the plain-language trial descriptions
5. If something looks relevant, use the eligibility checker or note the trial details to discuss with your specialist

You don't need to sign up. You don't need a referral. You're just looking.

---

**H2: Questions to ask your specialist**

If you find a trial that seems relevant, these questions are worth bringing to your next appointment:

- "Is there any research currently open for someone at my stage of scleroderma?"
- "Do you know anything about [trial name]?"
- "Would participating in this trial interfere with my current treatment plan?"
- "Are there scleroderma specialist centres near me that run trials?"
- "What are the main risks, in your view, for someone with my profile?"

Your specialist should welcome these questions. If a particular trial isn't suitable for you, they may know of others.

---

**H2: Frequently Asked Questions**

**Q: Can I join a trial if I'm already taking immunosuppressants?**
Some scleroderma trials specifically want participants on background immunosuppression — it depends on the trial design. Always check the eligibility criteria and discuss with your specialist.

**Q: Are there scleroderma trials available in Australia?**
Yes. TrialScreen lists Australian trials as well as international studies with sites in Australia. Some international trials have Australian sites that are recruiting. Scleroderma Australia can also point you to relevant research networks.

**Q: My scleroderma is fairly mild. Are there trials for me?**
Not all scleroderma trials are for people with severe disease. Some specifically want participants with early or limited scleroderma. Search TrialScreen and use the eligibility information to see what matches your situation.

**Q: Will I have to travel overseas for a scleroderma trial?**
Most trials are designed to be conducted locally. If a trial is international, it will typically have sites in multiple countries, including Australia when relevant.

**Q: What happens to my data?**
Your data is subject to strict ethics and privacy protections. Research teams are required to de-identify data and can only use it for the purposes outlined in the consent form.

---

**Related articles:**

- "Rare Disease Day: What It Means and Why It Matters"

title: BA-003
subtitle: Blog Articles
body:
**Headline:** Menopause Research: What's Actually Being Studied Right Now

**URL slug:** /resources/menopause-research-clinical-trials

**Meta description:** Menopause research is finally catching up. Here's a plain-language overview of what's being studied for menopause and perimenopause — and how to find studies near you.

**Author:** TrialScreen Editorial Team | **Date:** [Publication date] | **Read time:** 10 min

**Primary keyword:** menopause research clinical trials
**Secondary keywords:** perimenopause studies, menopause treatment research, hot flashes research, menopause hormone therapy trials

---

**Introduction**

Menopause affects approximately 1.3 billion women globally by 2025. And yet, for decades, it was underfunded, under-researched, and frequently dismissed.

Women were told hot flashes were just part of life. Brain fog was called anxiety. Sleep disruption was attributed to stress. The research that did exist was often conducted without enough women, or without adequate attention to how menopause specifically contributed to what was being studied.

That's changing.

In the last decade — and especially in the last five years — menopause has become a serious research priority. Dedicated studies. Specialist clinics. Long-term cohort projects.

This article is a plain-language overview of what's actually being studied for menopause and perimenopause right now, and how you can find research options that may be relevant to your experience.

---

**H2: First — what does "menopause research" actually cover?**

Menopause isn't one symptom. It's a transition that can span a decade or more, affecting almost every system in the body. Research reflects that breadth.

Active areas of study include:

- **Vasomotor symptoms** (hot flashes, night sweats)
- **Sleep disruption**
- **Cognitive changes** (brain fog, memory, concentration)
- **Mental health** (anxiety, depression, mood changes)
- **Bone health** (osteoporosis risk increases post-menopause)
- **Cardiovascular health** (oestrogen decline affects heart and vascular health)
- **Genitourinary symptoms** (vaginal dryness, urinary urgency)
- **Sexual health and libido**
- **Weight and metabolic changes**

Research is happening in all of these areas. Some studies focus on treatment. Others focus on understanding the mechanisms. Some look at preventive approaches.

---

**H2: What are researchers testing right now?**

*H3: Hormone therapy — new formulations and delivery methods*

Hormone replacement therapy (HRT) — now more commonly called menopausal hormone therapy (MHT) — has been used for decades to manage menopause symptoms. But research into MHT hasn't stood still.

Researchers are currently studying:
- Different formulations of oestrogen and progesterone
- Body-identical (bioidentical) hormones versus synthetic versions
- Transdermal (patch or gel) delivery versus oral
- Whether starting MHT earlier in perimenopause produces different outcomes than starting later
- Long-term cardiovascular safety profiles

The ESTEEM study (a real ongoing study) is among those looking at MHT combinations for women with moderate to severe menopausal symptoms.

*H3: Non-hormonal approaches to vasomotor symptoms*

Not everyone can use hormone therapy — due to personal choice, a history of certain cancers, or other medical factors. This has driven growing research interest in non-hormonal options.

Recent focus includes:
- **Fezolinetant:** a non-hormonal medication targeting brain pathways that regulate body temperature. Phase 3 trials showed it could significantly reduce hot flash frequency and severity.
- **Stellate ganglion block:** a nerve block procedure being studied for its effect on hot flashes.
- **Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) adaptations** for menopause-related anxiety and sleep.

*H3: Brain health and cognition*

One of the most under-acknowledged aspects of menopause is its effect on cognitive function. "Brain fog" — the difficulty concentrating and remembering words that many women describe — is now being taken seriously in research.

Studies are examining whether oestrogen's role in brain function explains cognitive changes during menopause, and whether timing of MHT initiation affects cognitive outcomes in the long term.

*H3: Bone density*

Oestrogen plays a significant role in maintaining bone density. Post-menopausal osteoporosis is a significant public health issue. Research is studying not just pharmacological interventions but lifestyle factors — exercise, nutrition — and whether early intervention during perimenopause changes long-term bone health outcomes.

*H3: Lifestyle interventions*

Several trials are studying whether specific dietary patterns, exercise programmes, and sleep interventions could meaningfully reduce menopause symptoms — either on their own or alongside medical treatments.

---

**H2: The equity gap in menopause research**

An important caveat: much of the existing menopause research has been conducted on white, Western, and post-menopausal (rather than perimenopausal) women.

This means findings may not apply equally across all ethnic backgrounds, and the perimenopause stage — which can begin in a woman's 40s and involves significant hormonal fluctuation — has historically been under-studied.

Newer research is working to address this. If you come from a background that's been under-represented in previous research, some studies may specifically be looking for participants like you.

---

**H2: How to find menopause research relevant to you**

TrialScreen lists menopause and perimenopause research studies from multiple registries, rewritten in plain language.

To search:
1. Visit [TrialScreen.org](https://trialscreen.org)
2. Search "menopause," "perimenopause," "hot flashes," or a specific symptom you're experiencing
3. Filter by location and whether the trial requires clinic visits
4. Read the plain-language summary of each trial
5. If something seems relevant, note the details and bring them to your GP or specialist

---

**H2: Questions to ask your GP or gynaecologist**

If you find a study you're curious about:

- "I've heard there are trials looking at menopause treatments — is any of this relevant to my situation?"
- "I found a study on TrialScreen about [topic]. Is this something I'd be eligible for?"
- "I'm interested in whether there's anything beyond what I'm already doing for [specific symptom]. Is research an option?"

---

**H2: Frequently Asked Questions**

**Q: I'm in perimenopause, not yet fully menopausal. Are there trials for me?**
Yes. Many trials specifically want participants in perimenopause, because this is when hormonal changes begin. Check the eligibility criteria — they'll specify the stage.

**Q: I had breast cancer. Can I still join menopause research?**
Some trials exclude people with a history of hormone-sensitive cancers. Others are specifically designed for people in this situation. Search TrialScreen and check eligibility requirements carefully; your oncologist can also advise.

**Q: Do I have to stop my current HRT to join a trial?**
Not necessarily. Some trials want participants who are already on HRT, others want those who haven't used it. The trial description will specify.

**Q: Where can I find menopause specialist support outside of a trial?**
Your GP is the starting point. Referral to a menopause specialist or gynaecologist with a menopause focus may be appropriate. In Australia, the Australasian Menopause Society maintains a directory of practitioners.

---

**Related articles:**
- "Clinical Trials Through Every Decade of Life"
- "How to Talk to Your Doctor About Clinical Trials"
- "Women's Health: What Research Is Prioritising Now"

**External resources:**
- [Australasian Menopause Society](https://www.menopause.org.au)
- [British Menopause Society](https://thebms.org.uk)
- [Menopause Society (formerly NAMS, US)](https://menopause.org)

**CTA:** Search menopause research studies in plain language at [TrialScreen.org](https://trialscreen.org). Free. No sign-up required. You decide what to do next.

title: BA-004
subtitle: Blog Articles
body:
**Headline:** Decentralised Clinical Trials: What It Means to Participate from Home

**URL slug:** /resources/decentralised-clinical-trials-participate-from-home

**Meta description:** Some clinical trials no longer require clinic visits. Here's what decentralised and home-based trials look like, and how to find them.

**Author:** TrialScreen Editorial Team | **Date:** [Publication date] | **Read time:** 8 min

**Primary keyword:** decentralised clinical trials
**Secondary keywords:** home-based clinical trials, remote clinical research, telehealth research studies, virtual clinical trials

---

**Introduction**

For most of the history of clinical research, participating in a trial meant one thing: getting yourself to a clinic. Regularly. Often a specialist clinic far from home.

For people who lived near a major research hospital, this was manageable. For everyone else — people in rural and regional areas, people who couldn't take time off work, people with disabilities, people caring for family members — it was often impossible.

This created a problem in research. The people most easily able to participate in trials were not always the most representative of the population. Which meant research findings didn't always apply to the full range of people who needed them.

Decentralised clinical trials — also called DCTs, virtual trials, or home-based trials — are designed to change that.

---

**H2: What is a decentralised clinical trial?**

A decentralised clinical trial is one where some or all of the research activities happen away from a traditional clinic or hospital. Instead of travelling to a research site, participants may:

- Receive study medications or devices delivered to their home
- Complete check-ins via telehealth (video or phone)
- Use a study app on their phone to record symptoms, submit questionnaires, or track data
- Have blood samples or other tests taken at a local GP clinic
- Use wearable devices (like smartwatches) to passively collect health data

Some trials are "fully decentralised" — meaning there are no required clinic visits at all. Others are "hybrid" — a small number of in-person visits for key assessments, with the rest done remotely.

---

**H2: What types of conditions use decentralised trials?**

Decentralised trial formats have been used across a wide range of conditions, including:

- Skin conditions (eczema, psoriasis) — where assessments can be done via photograph and questionnaire
- Mental health (depression, anxiety) — where app-based check-ins and video consultations work well
- Chronic pain conditions — where symptom tracking via app replaces frequent clinic visits
- Respiratory conditions — where spirometry can be done at a local GP
- Rare diseases — where the patient population is geographically dispersed

The COVID-19 pandemic significantly accelerated the adoption of decentralised trial designs, as researchers were forced to adapt existing trials to remote delivery. Many of those adaptations proved effective — and have stayed.

---

**H2: What are the advantages for participants?**

*H3: Reduced travel burden*

The most obvious advantage is that you don't have to travel. For people living outside major cities, or people whose condition makes travel difficult, this can be the difference between being able to participate and not.

*H3: Flexibility*

Telehealth check-ins can often be scheduled outside business hours. App-based data collection happens on your own schedule. This makes participation more compatible with work and family life.

*H3: More representative research*

When research includes people from more diverse geographical, socioeconomic, and health backgrounds, the findings are more useful for everyone. Decentralised trials help make this possible.

*H3: Reduced disruption to daily life*

Multiple clinic visits over months can be disruptive. Decentralised formats typically ask less of your time and create less disruption to your routine.

---

**H2: Are decentralised trials as rigorous as traditional ones?**

Yes — when well-designed, decentralised trials follow the same scientific and ethical standards as traditional trials. They go through the same ethics review process, use the same informed consent procedures, and collect the same quality of data.

The methods of data collection are different. The standards are not.

That said, not every condition or research question is suited to a fully decentralised format. Some assessments genuinely require specialist equipment or clinical expertise. Researchers think carefully about which components of a trial can be moved remote and which can't.

---

**H2: What does the participant experience look like?**

Here's a typical flow for a hybrid decentralised trial:

1. **Discovery:** You find the trial on TrialScreen, read the plain-language description, and check the eligibility criteria.
2. **Expression of interest:** You complete a short online form or call the research team.
3. **Screening:** A video call with the research team, followed by a local blood test (organised by the team).
4. **Baseline visit:** One in-person clinic visit to establish starting measurements.
5. **Treatment phase:** Study medication delivered to your home monthly. Weekly symptom questionnaire via app. Monthly 20-minute telehealth check-in.
6. **Final visit:** One in-person clinic visit at the end of the treatment phase.
7. **Follow-up:** Quarterly app questionnaires for 12 months after treatment.

---

**H2: How to find home-based trials on TrialScreen**

When you search TrialScreen, you can use filters to identify trials with home-based or decentralised components:

1. Go to [TrialScreen.org](https://trialscreen.org)
2. Search your condition
3. Look for the "home-based" or "remote" filter
4. Read the participation requirements in each trial's description

If you're unsure whether a trial can accommodate your location, the trial description will usually include contact details for the research team — you can ask before committing to anything.

---

**H2: Frequently Asked Questions**

**Q: Are decentralised trials available in Australia?**
Yes. Decentralised trial adoption in Australia has grown significantly since 2020. TrialScreen lists Australian studies with home-based components.

**Q: Do I need a particular type of phone or device?**
It depends on the trial. Most app-based trials use standard smartphones. Some provide wearable devices. The trial description will specify any equipment requirements.

**Q: What if I have trouble with technology?**
Research teams are aware that not all participants are tech-savvy. Most decentralised trial platforms are designed to be simple to use, and teams provide support. If technology is a barrier, mention it during the screening call — they may have alternatives.

**Q: Does "decentralised" mean I'll never need to see a doctor in person?**
Not always. Many hybrid trials still have one or two in-person visits. Fully remote trials do exist, but they're not universal. The trial description will be clear about what's required.

---

**Related articles:**
- "5 Things That Actually Happen in a Clinical Trial"
- "How to Talk to Your Doctor About Clinical Trials"
- "Clinical Trials Through Every Decade of Life"

**CTA:** Search home-based and decentralised trials at [TrialScreen.org](https://trialscreen.org) — plain language, free, no sign-up.

title: BA-005
subtitle: Blog Articles
body:
**Headline:** How to Talk to Your Doctor About Clinical Trials

**URL slug:** /resources/how-to-talk-to-doctor-about-clinical-trials

**Meta description:** Want to ask your doctor about clinical trials but not sure how? This plain-language guide gives you the exact questions and phrases to use.

**Author:** TrialScreen Editorial Team | **Date:** [Publication date] | **Read time:** 7 min

**Primary keyword:** how to talk to doctor about clinical trials
**Secondary keywords:** asking about clinical trials, clinical trial conversation, patient-doctor communication research

---

**Introduction**

You've done the search. You've read the description. You've thought about it for a week.

And now you're sitting in your doctor's waiting room, wondering how to bring it up without sounding like you've been self-diagnosing on the internet at midnight.

(Which, for the record, is completely valid. Informed patients are better patients.)

This guide is for the moment between finding a trial and talking to someone about it. It gives you the exact questions to ask, the language that tends to work, and a few things to keep in mind so the conversation goes well.

---

**H2: Why it can feel hard to bring up**

Many patients hesitate to mention clinical trials to their doctors for a few reasons:

- Fear of seeming like they're questioning their doctor's judgment
- Not wanting to seem "desperate" or like they've "given up"
- Uncertainty about whether it's even relevant to bring up
- Not knowing the right words

Here's the truth: most doctors welcome patients who are engaged in their own care. Asking about a clinical trial isn't a challenge to your doctor's authority — it's a sign that you're taking your health seriously.

And even if the specific trial you found isn't right for you, raising the topic can open a broader conversation about what options exist.

---

**H2: Before the appointment — what to bring**

*H3: Print or save the trial summary*

If you found a trial on TrialScreen, the plain-language summary is your starting point. Print it or have it ready on your phone. Being able to point to a specific trial makes the conversation concrete.

*H3: Write down your questions*

Doctors work quickly, and appointments are short. Writing down your questions beforehand means you won't forget them when you're under the pressure of a 10-minute appointment.

*H3: Know the basic details*

It helps to know: the name of the condition being studied, the phase of the trial (if listed), and whether the trial is still recruiting. TrialScreen's trial pages include this information in plain language.

---

**H2: How to start the conversation**

These are opening lines that work well:

- *"I've been reading about some research being done for [condition]. I found something I wanted to ask you about."*
- *"I read about a clinical trial for [condition] — would this be something worth looking into for my situation?"*
- *"I know trials aren't always the right choice, but I wanted to ask whether there's anything being studied that might be relevant to me."*
- *"I found this on TrialScreen — it's a plain-language trial database. Can I show you what I found?"*

None of these are aggressive. They signal curiosity and openness, not a demand for a specific outcome.

---

**H2: Questions to ask about a specific trial**

Once you've raised the topic, these are useful follow-up questions:

- *"Do you know anything about this trial?"*
- *"Based on my current health profile, would I likely be eligible?"*
- *"Would joining this trial affect my existing treatment in any way?"*
- *"Are there specialist centres you'd recommend who run scleroderma [or: relevant condition] trials?"*
- *"If this trial isn't right for me, do you know of others that might be?"*

---

**H2: Broader questions about clinical trials**

Even if you don't have a specific trial in mind, these questions can open the conversation:

- *"Is there any clinical research happening right now for people at my stage of [condition]?"*
- *"Would you ever recommend a clinical trial as an option for someone like me?"*
- *"What should I look for when evaluating whether a trial is right for me?"*

---

**H2: If your doctor is dismissive**

It's rare, but some doctors are unfamiliar with current research in their area, or aren't supportive of patient-initiated trial enquiries.

If this happens:

- Ask if they can refer you to a specialist who does see clinical research patients
- Contact the research team directly using the contact details in the trial listing — they can often advise on whether your GP would need to be involved
- Seek a second opinion if you feel your interest isn't being taken seriously

---

**H2: What a good response looks like**

A supportive doctor might say:

- "Yes, I know that trial — let me pull up what I know about eligibility."
- "I'm not familiar with it, but I can look into it and let you know."
- "That sounds like something worth discussing with your specialist."
- "I don't think that specific trial is right for you, but there's another one I've seen mentioned that might be."

Any of these is a good starting point. You don't need an immediate yes — you need an open door.

---

**H2: After the appointment**

If your doctor agrees to look into a trial or refer you, follow up. Appointments are short, and things can fall through the cracks.

A brief message through your patient portal, or a call to the clinic: *"Following up on the clinical trial we discussed — I wanted to check whether there's any update."*

---

**H2: Frequently Asked Questions**

**Q: Do I need a GP referral to join a clinical trial?**
Not always. Many trials allow self-referral — you can contact the research team directly. However, some trials require your treating doctor to be informed, and it's generally good practice to keep your GP in the loop.

**Q: What if my doctor doesn't know about the trial I found?**
That's common. Your doctor can't be familiar with every active trial. It's completely reasonable to share the TrialScreen summary and ask them to review it. If they're interested, they can contact the trial team themselves.

**Q: What if the trial is interstate or overseas?**
Your GP can still help you evaluate whether you might be eligible. If the trial is conducted remotely or has satellite sites, the research team can advise on logistics.

---

**Related articles:**
- "5 Things That Actually Happen in a Clinical Trial"
- "Decentralised Clinical Trials: What It Means to Participate from Home"
- "Scleroderma Clinical Trials: A Complete Guide"

**CTA:** Ready to start the search? Find 120,000+ trials in plain language at [TrialScreen.org](https://trialscreen.org).

---

title: BA-006
subtitle: Blog Articles
body:
**Headline:** Clinical Trials Through Every Decade of Life

**URL slug:** /resources/clinical-trials-every-age

**Meta description:** Clinical trials aren't just for older adults. From children to people in their 80s, research is happening for every life stage. Here's what it looks like — decade by decade.

**Author:** TrialScreen Editorial Team | **Date:** [Publication date] | **Read time:** 9 min

**Primary keyword:** clinical trials every age group
**Secondary keywords:** paediatric clinical trials, older adults research, clinical trials for children, research for seniors

---

**Introduction**

When most people picture a clinical trial participant, they picture an older adult in a hospital setting.

That's an outdated picture.

Clinical trials recruit participants of every age — from newborns to people in their 90s. The conditions being studied, the types of trials available, and the practicalities of participation vary significantly by life stage.

This article takes you through what clinical research looks like across the decades of life. Whether you're searching for yourself, for a child, for an ageing parent, or out of curiosity — this is designed to be useful.

---

**H2: Childhood and Adolescence (Ages 0–17)**

*H3: Paediatric trials: why they matter and why they're limited*

For most of medical history, children were excluded from clinical research. The intention was protective — children couldn't consent for themselves, and researchers worried about exposing them to risk.

The result was that most medications given to children were approved based on adult data, with paediatric doses calculated by guesswork. This was its own kind of risk.

Regulatory frameworks have shifted. In most developed countries, pharmaceutical companies are now required or incentivised to conduct paediatric trials for new treatments. The field of paediatric clinical research has grown substantially.

*H3: What trials look like for children and teens*

For young children, a parent or guardian provides consent. For adolescents (typically 12 and over), the child also provides "assent" — a process of agreeing to participate in an age-appropriate way.

Paediatric trials exist for a wide range of conditions: childhood cancers, rare genetic diseases, mental health conditions, neurodevelopmental conditions, and more.

The Arts for the Blues study is an example of a paediatric-adjacent mental health study looking at whether creative therapies could support young people experiencing depression. This kind of study — more lifestyle and wellbeing focused than pharmaceutical — is an increasingly common format for young participant research.

---

**H2: Young Adults (Ages 18–35)**

*H3: The missed demographic in research*

Young adults are often considered healthy and are sometimes excluded from disease-focused trials. But this age group has significant research relevance:

- Mental health conditions often first present in the 18–25 age range
- Autoimmune conditions (including scleroderma, lupus, MS) often first present in young women in their 20s and 30s
- Reproductive health research (fertility, contraception, endometriosis) centres on this age group
- Prevention and lifestyle intervention research often targets young adults before chronic conditions develop

*H3: Key areas of research for this group*

- Mental health: depression, anxiety, psychosis
- Autoimmune diseases
- Reproductive health
- Healthy lifestyle and prevention studies

Young adults are also heavily recruited as healthy volunteers in early-phase trials — studies that need healthy participants to establish safety profiles. These can involve time commitments (often in-clinic stays) but are typically well-compensated.

---

**H2: Middle Age (Ages 36–55)**

*H3: The peak of chronic disease onset*

Middle age is when many chronic conditions first present — type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, early cancer screenings, autoimmune conditions, and — for many women — perimenopause.

Research in this age group is abundant. Conditions like obesity, metabolic syndrome, pre-diabetes, and hypertension are major areas of study, reflecting their prevalence.

*H3: Menopause and perimenopause research*

As covered in our dedicated article, menopause research has expanded significantly. Women in their 40s and early 50s are a key demographic for perimenopause and early menopause studies.

*H3: Cancer screening and early-stage cancer*

Middle age is also when many cancer diagnoses occur. Research into early detection, treatment decisions, and quality-of-life during treatment recruits heavily from this age group.

---

**H2: Older Adults (Ages 56–75)**

*H3: The most enrolled group*

Older adults are, statistically, the most enrolled age group in clinical trials — reflecting the higher prevalence of the conditions most studied (cardiovascular disease, cancer, diabetes, neurological conditions).

But there's a paradox: the oldest old (75+) are often under-represented in trials, meaning drugs approved based on trials in 55–70 year olds may have different effects in people who are 80 or 85.

*H3: Key research areas*

- Heart failure and cardiovascular disease
- Type 2 diabetes management
- Oncology (cancer treatment and supportive care)
- Bone health and osteoporosis
- Cognitive decline and early Alzheimer's research
- Falls prevention and functional independence

*H3: What participation looks like*

Older adult trial participants often have complex health profiles — multiple conditions, multiple medications. Research teams screen carefully to understand these interactions. Decentralised and hybrid trial formats have expanded access for older adults who find frequent travel difficult.

---

**H2: Later Life (Ages 76+)**

*H3: Why this group has been under-represented*

Older adults aged 75 and above have historically been excluded from trials due to:
- Multiple medications (polypharmacy) complicating interpretation
- Frailty and concurrent health issues
- Concerns about travel and compliance

The result is that a significant and growing population segment — people living into their 80s and 90s — has limited research evidence specific to their profiles.

This is now a recognised gap. Researchers are beginning to design studies that specifically include and even focus on the oldest age groups.

*H3: What's being studied*

- Dementia and Alzheimer's disease (the Lombardy study and similar research)
- Frailty and functional decline
- Quality of life in older adults with multiple chronic conditions
- Palliative and supportive care approaches

*H3: Role of caregivers*

For older adults who find it difficult to navigate trial information themselves, family members and caregivers can search TrialScreen on their behalf and discuss options with treating physicians.

---

**H2: Frequently Asked Questions**

**Q: Is there an upper age limit for clinical trials?**
Many trials have upper age limits written into their eligibility criteria, but this varies widely. Some trials have no upper age limit. Search TrialScreen and check the eligibility criteria for each study.

**Q: Are there trials specifically for healthy people of any age?**
Yes — Phase 1 trials and many lifestyle/prevention studies recruit healthy volunteers. These often pay a participation fee. Search "healthy volunteer" on TrialScreen.

**Q: How do I find paediatric trials for my child?**
Search TrialScreen and filter by paediatric or enter your child's condition. Look for trials specifying the relevant age range. Your child's specialist is also a key source of information about open paediatric studies.

**Q: My father is 82 and has Alzheimer's — are there trials available?**
Yes. Alzheimer's and dementia research recruits older participants, though eligibility varies by stage of disease and other health factors. TrialScreen lists Alzheimer's studies; a specialist at a memory clinic can also advise.

---

**Related articles:**
- "5 Things That Actually Happen in a Clinical Trial"
- "Menopause Research: What's Actually Being Studied Right Now"
- "Scleroderma Clinical Trials: A Complete Guide"

**CTA:** Search for trials relevant to your life stage at [TrialScreen.org](https://trialscreen.org) — free, plain language, no sign-up.

title: PV-001 · Sarah (pseudonym) | Age 38 | Systemic sclerosis, diffuse type | Brisbane, Australia | Participated in a Phase 2 skin fibrosis trial
subtitle: Sarah (pseudonym) | Age 38 | Systemic sclerosis, diffuse type | Brisbane, Australia | Participated in a Phase 2 skin fibrosis trial
body:
**[REQUIRES CONSENT — All names, locations, and identifying details are anonymised placeholders. This piece must not be published until a signed HIPAA-equivalent consent form is on file for the real subject, or the piece is explicitly labelled as a composite/illustrative narrative.]**

**Headline:** "I Didn't Think Research Was for Someone Like Me": Sarah's Journey with Scleroderma

**Subject:** Sarah (pseudonym) | Age 38 | Systemic sclerosis, diffuse type | Brisbane, Australia | Participated in a Phase 2 skin fibrosis trial

**Publication:** TrialScreen.org/patient-voices | **Date:** [Publication date] | **Read time:** 10 min

---

**Introduction**

Sarah was 34 when a rheumatologist said a word she'd never heard before.

"She said 'scleroderma' and I nodded like I understood," Sarah recalls. "Then I went home and spent the next week on the internet, going down every rabbit hole I could find."

What she found was a mix of medical literature written for specialists, outdated patient forums, and a diagnosis that felt like a wall.

Four years later, Sarah is eight months into a clinical trial studying a new approach to managing skin fibrosis in systemic sclerosis. She's clear that she doesn't know what the trial will show. But she's equally clear about what searching for it gave her.

"Knowing the options exist changed everything," she says. "Even before I joined anything."

---

**Q&A**

**TrialScreen: How would you describe scleroderma to someone who's never heard of it?**

Sarah: I tell people it's like your body produces too much scar tissue — and it does it from the inside out. My skin started to thicken on my hands and forearms first. Then the fatigue hit. The Raynaud's. The way cold affects your hands is hard to describe unless you've experienced it.

The difficult part is that everyone's disease behaves differently. You can have two people both diagnosed with diffuse scleroderma and have very different experiences.

**TrialScreen: What was the period after diagnosis like?**

Sarah: Honestly, it felt like a narrowing. You go from feeling like your future is open to feeling like there's a set of walls around you. My rheumatologist is fantastic, but she's managing my disease — she's not studying it. I appreciated her care enormously, but I wanted to know: who is working on this? Is anyone looking for better answers?

**TrialScreen: When did you first start looking at research options?**

Sarah: About 18 months after diagnosis. I'd been managing fairly well, but I was aware that my condition could progress, and I felt like I was just waiting. A friend whose husband has a different rare disease mentioned TrialScreen. I wasn't expecting much.

**TrialScreen: What did you find when you searched?**

Sarah: I typed "scleroderma" and there were actual studies. In plain language. I'd tried ClinicalTrials.gov before and come away more confused than when I started. TrialScreen was different — I could actually understand what was being studied and roughly whether I might fit.

I found four studies. Two were in the US and not open to Australians. One had a narrow eligibility window I didn't meet. But one — the one I'm in now — was open, at a hospital in Brisbane, and seemed to match my situation.

**TrialScreen: What made you decide to look into it further?**

Sarah: I'm a pragmatist. I read the description a few times. I looked up the research team. I talked to my rheumatologist about it. She knew of the trial, which felt reassuring — like I hadn't just found something obscure. She said she thought it was worth exploring, but made clear it was entirely my decision.

That mattered. The fact that she didn't push me — that actually made me more willing to go and look.

**TrialScreen: What was the screening process like?**

Sarah: A phone call first, with a research nurse. She went through eligibility criteria, asked about my medications and the stage of my disease, and explained what the trial involved. I felt like I could ask anything. I wasn't pressured at all.

Then a clinic visit for blood tests, a skin score assessment — they measure skin involvement on a scale — and an echocardiogram. That all took about a half day.

**TrialScreen: Were you worried about the risks?**

Sarah: Yes. Honestly. This is a medication that's been used in other autoimmune conditions, but hasn't been specifically studied in scleroderma in this formulation. They were very clear about the unknown elements.

What helped was that the consent process took time. I didn't feel like they were trying to rush me into anything. I took the consent form home, showed it to my GP, and came back with questions. They answered all of them.

**TrialScreen: You were randomised. Do you know what group you're in?**

Sarah: I don't. It's blinded. Some days I'm convinced I'm on the active drug because I've noticed small changes. Other days I think I'm imagining things. I've learned to hold that uncertainty more easily than I expected.

**TrialScreen: What does participation involve day-to-day?**

Sarah: I take a tablet every day — either the study drug or a placebo, I don't know which. Monthly clinic visits, which are quite thorough: blood tests, skin assessment, questionnaire about symptoms and quality of life. The research team is attentive. If I have a concern, I can message them.

**TrialScreen: What would you tell someone with scleroderma who's wondering whether to look at research?**

Sarah: I'd say: looking costs nothing. You don't have to commit to anything. You don't have to know in advance whether it's right for you. Just see what exists.

And if your instinct is "this isn't for me" — that's completely valid. I had a friend with lupus who looked and decided she wasn't interested in any of the trials she found. And that's fine. The point is knowing you had a choice.

**TrialScreen: Is there anything you wish someone had told you earlier?**

Sarah: That research isn't for extraordinary people. It's for curious people. I spent 18 months thinking trials were for people who were more ill than me, or more brave, or more something. I was wrong. It was just about asking the question.

---

**Pull Quotes:**

> *"Knowing the options exist changed everything. Even before I joined anything."*

> *"Looking costs nothing. You don't have to commit to anything."*

> *"I spent 18 months thinking trials were for people who were more brave than me. I was wrong."*

---

**Editorial Note**

Sarah's story is her own. It doesn't represent a typical trial experience, and it makes no claims about the outcomes of the study she joined. The trial she participated in is ongoing; results have not been published.

If you have scleroderma and want to explore what research is available, TrialScreen lists scleroderma studies in plain language at [TrialScreen.org](https://trialscreen.org). Searching is free and carries no obligation.

title: PV-002 · Priya (pseudonym) | Parent | Son Marcus (pseudonym), age 14 at time of study | Sydney, Australia | Arts for the Blues creative therapy programme study
subtitle: Priya (pseudonym) | Parent | Son Marcus (pseudonym), age 14 at time of study | Sydney, Australia | Arts for the Blues creative therapy programme study
body:
**[REQUIRES CONSENT — all names and identifying details are placeholders. Parental consent and child's assent (where applicable) required. Do not publish without signed release on file.]**

**Headline:** "He Started Talking Again": Priya on Her Son Marcus and the Arts for the Blues Programme

**Subject:** Priya (pseudonym) | Parent | Son Marcus (pseudonym), age 14 at time of study | Sydney, Australia | Arts for the Blues creative therapy programme study

**Publication:** TrialScreen.org/patient-voices | **Date:** [Publication date] | **Read time:** 10 min

---

**Introduction**

Priya remembers the exact week everything changed.

"Three phone calls from the school in five days," she says. "Marcus wasn't eating properly. He wasn't sleeping. He was withdrawing from everything he used to love — football, his friends, even gaming."

Marcus was 14. He'd been quiet for months before that week, but Priya had thought it was adolescence. The school calls clarified that something more was happening.

What followed was months of GP appointments, referrals, and waitlists. The mental health system for young people, Priya will tell you without bitterness but without softening it, was not built for the speed at which families need help.

"I felt like I was failing him," she says. "Not because I wasn't trying. Because everything I tried took months."

Then, through an online parenting group, someone mentioned TrialScreen. Priya searched "teenager depression" — not expecting anything. She found the Arts for the Blues study, a programme testing whether creative therapies could support young people aged 12–18 experiencing depression.

Marcus is 17 now. He describes himself as "mostly okay." He has a sketchbook he carries everywhere.

---

**Q&A**

**TrialScreen: Tell us a bit about what Marcus was like before things changed.**

Priya: He was always sensitive, creative. He loved drawing from when he was tiny. Football on the weekends. He had a good group of friends. He was the kid who always wanted to make everyone feel included.

That didn't disappear when he started struggling. But it went underground.

**TrialScreen: How did the system respond initially?**

Priya: The GP was wonderful — she referred us to a psychologist immediately. Three months waiting. We got on the waitlist for a community mental health service — five months. I had a child who was struggling now, and I was being told to wait.

I don't fault the individual clinicians. The system is overwhelmed. But I was a desperate parent.

**TrialScreen: How did you find out about the Arts for the Blues study?**

Priya: A woman in a private Facebook group for parents of teenagers with mental health challenges mentioned TrialScreen. She said she'd found a study for her son through it. I searched the next morning.

I typed in "teenager depression" and "young person mental health" and a few other things. The Arts for the Blues study came up. The description was in plain language — I could actually understand what they were studying, which was a relief.

**TrialScreen: What did the study involve?**

Priya: Marcus was assessed first, to confirm he met the eligibility criteria — which included a certain level of depression severity measured on a standard scale. The research team was clear with me that the programme was being studied, not proven. I appreciated that honesty.

The programme involved weekly group sessions — creative arts activities led by trained facilitators. Drama, visual art, music. Marcus was sceptical at first. Very sceptical. He's a teenage boy. The idea of sitting in a room making art with strangers did not appeal.

**TrialScreen: How did his first session go?**

Priya: He came home and said "it was okay." Which from Marcus, at that stage, was something.

By the third week, he mentioned that there was another boy in the group who was also into drawing. They'd talked. I nearly cried at the kitchen table.

**TrialScreen: What changed over the course of the programme?**

Priya: Gradually, the sketchbook appeared. He started drawing again at home — just quietly, by himself. He started sleeping a bit better. He was still anxious, still had hard days. I don't want to overstate it. He was also seeing a psychologist by that point — the waitlist had finally cleared.

But something in him seemed to have more room in it. Less compressed.

**TrialScreen: As a parent, how did you feel about being part of a research study?**

Priya: It's a strange thing to hold. You're hoping your child's experience contributes to something that helps other kids. But mostly you're just hoping your child gets through the week.

I was very clear with the research team: I'm here for Marcus. If at any point this isn't working for him, we leave. They completely respected that.

**TrialScreen: Marcus is 17 now. What would he say about the programme, in his own words?**

Priya: I asked him if he'd mind me sharing his perspective, and he said yes, that's fine, as long as I didn't embarrass him. [Laughs.]

He says the part that helped most was just being with other people who were also struggling, and not having to talk about it directly. The art gave him something to do with his hands and his head while he was in the room. "It was less intense than therapy," he said. "I could just be there."

**TrialScreen: What would you tell a parent in a similar situation?**

Priya: I would say: you're allowed to look for more than one thing at once. The waitlist and the research study weren't mutually exclusive. We were on both.

I would also say: you know your child. If your instinct says something is worth looking into, trust that. You're not being reckless by searching. You're being a parent.

**TrialScreen: Anything else you want families to know?**

Priya: That it's okay if you look and find nothing, or find something that doesn't apply to you. I searched for other conditions Marcus had been assessed for and found nothing. That was disappointing, but it wasn't failure. I looked, and I knew.

With the arts programme, I happened to look and find something. And that something happened to be available at the right moment.

---

**Pull Quotes:**

> *"He came home and said 'it was okay.' Which from Marcus, at that stage, was something."*

> *"You're allowed to look for more than one thing at once. The waitlist and the research study weren't mutually exclusive."*

> *"You're not being reckless by searching. You're being a parent."*

---

**Editorial Note**

Priya and Marcus's story is their own. No claims are made about the effectiveness of the Arts for the Blues programme — the study is designed to find out whether creative therapies could support young people experiencing depression; its results are pending publication.

If you have a young person in your family experiencing mental health challenges, TrialScreen lists mental health research studies for young people at [TrialScreen.org](https://trialscreen.org). Searching carries no obligation.

If you are in crisis, please contact your GP or local mental health crisis service immediately. In Australia: Kids Helpline 1800 55 1800. Lifeline 13 11 14.

title: PV-003 · Helen (pseudonym) | Age 53 | Perimenopause/menopause | Melbourne, Australia | Found menopause research via TrialScreen; currently in a non-hormonal symptom management study
subtitle: Helen (pseudonym) | Age 53 | Perimenopause/menopause | Melbourne, Australia | Found menopause research via TrialScreen; currently in a non-hormonal symptom management study
body:
**[REQUIRES CONSENT — all names and identifying details are placeholders. Do not publish without signed release on file.]**

**Headline:** "I Thought I Just Had to Put Up with It": Helen on Menopause, Research, and Finding Out More Options Existed

**Subject:** Helen (pseudonym) | Age 53 | Perimenopause/menopause | Melbourne, Australia | Found menopause research via TrialScreen; currently in a non-hormonal symptom management study

**Publication:** TrialScreen.org/patient-voices | **Date:** [Publication date] | **Read time:** 9 min

---

**Introduction**

For two years, Helen woke up at 3am, soaking wet, heart racing, unable to get back to sleep.

"I assumed it was just menopause," she says. "Which it was. But I'd internalised this idea that menopause was something you endured. You got hot flashes, you were tired, your brain went foggy. And you waited for it to pass."

Helen is a secondary school teacher. She describes herself as someone who researches everything — curriculum approaches, professional development, her children's medical appointments. "But somehow I had a blind spot about my own health," she says.

When a colleague mentioned that there were research studies specifically looking at menopause — not just treating it with HRT, but actually studying it — Helen was surprised. She found TrialScreen. She searched "menopause."

She found fourteen studies. She is currently in one.

"I'm not saying everyone should do what I did," she is careful to say. "But I wish I'd known the options existed two years earlier."

---

**Q&A**

**TrialScreen: How would you describe what the perimenopause years have been like for you?**

Helen: Unpredictable. That's the word. I'd have a good month and think I'd turned a corner. Then a terrible fortnight. The hot flashes were bad at night — multiple times a night, some periods. But it was the cognitive stuff that hit me hardest professionally.

Standing in front of a class of 28 students and losing the thread of what you were saying. Searching for words. I've been teaching for 22 years. I know my material. But something was interfering with retrieval.

I didn't tell anyone at work. I was worried it would affect how I was perceived.

**TrialScreen: Had you spoken to your GP about it?**

Helen: Yes, in the first year. She was supportive and recommended hormone therapy. I tried it — low-dose oestrogen, transdermal. It helped with the night sweats, mostly. But I had some concerns about long-term use — I have a family history of breast cancer, and while my GP explained the risk profile carefully, I was still cautious.

I also found the cognitive symptoms were persisting even on HRT.

**TrialScreen: What prompted you to search TrialScreen?**

Helen: A colleague. She'd been through menopause five years before me and mentioned offhand that she'd read about a non-hormonal option being studied. She told me about TrialScreen.

I went home that evening. I searched "menopause." And I was stunned, frankly. There were studies looking at the exact things I'd been experiencing. Non-hormonal approaches to hot flashes. Studies on the cognitive changes. A study on sleep specifically for menopausal women.

I felt something I hadn't felt in a while about this topic: relief. Like — people are working on this. Seriously.

**TrialScreen: What did you do next?**

Helen: I went through each study description carefully. TrialScreen's plain language made a real difference — I could understand what was being tested and roughly whether I might be eligible, without needing a medical dictionary.

I identified two studies I wanted to ask my GP about. I printed the summaries.

**TrialScreen: How did your GP respond when you raised the studies?**

Helen: She was interested. She knew of one of them already and said she thought I'd likely be eligible. She wasn't familiar with the second one but looked it up while I was there.

She made clear it was entirely my choice. She didn't push in either direction. I appreciated that.

**TrialScreen: You're currently in a study. What does that involve?**

Helen: It's a non-hormonal study — I can't share the details of what's being tested without potentially affecting the blinding, but I'm in a randomised controlled trial. I take something every day, I keep a symptom diary on an app, and I have a telehealth check-in with the research nurse once a month.

The convenience surprised me. I expected clinic visits every other week. Most of it is on my phone.

**TrialScreen: Has your experience of symptoms changed since joining?**

Helen: I don't know. Honestly. I'm blinded — I don't know if I'm on the active drug or a comparator. I have noticed some things. But I'm also aware that knowing you're in a trial affects perception. I try not to read too much into my own experience.

What I can say is that I feel like I'm doing something. Which sounds small, but after two years of just enduring, it's significant.

**TrialScreen: What do you wish had been different about your earlier experience with the medical system?**

Helen: I wish the conversation had started differently. Not "here's your option." But "here are your options."

My GP is good. She offered what she knew. But research options weren't part of the conversation. And I think for a lot of women, they never are. We're told menopause is a natural transition — which it is — and somehow that becomes "there's nothing much to be done."

There's a lot to be done. People are working on it. We just don't always hear about it.

**TrialScreen: What would you say to a woman in perimenopause or menopause who's reading this?**

Helen: Search. Just search. You might not find anything relevant. You might find something that's entirely unsuitable for you. Or you might, like me, find fourteen things and end up in a study that's changed how you feel about your own health.

The information doesn't obligate you to do anything. But I regret not having it sooner.

---

**Pull Quotes:**

> *"I'd internalised this idea that menopause was something you endured. You got hot flashes, you were tired. And you waited for it to pass."*

> *"Not 'here's your option.' But 'here are your options.'"*

> *"The information doesn't obligate you to do anything. But I regret not having it sooner."*

---

**Editorial Note**

Helen's experience is her own. No claims are made about the effectiveness of the study she joined. The study is ongoing and results have not been published.

TrialScreen lists menopause research studies in plain language at [TrialScreen.org/resources/menopause](https://trialscreen.org). Searching is free and carries no obligation.

---

We're a community of over 40,000 people who believe the same thing: that everyone should be able to find and understand clinical research, without needing a medical degree, and without being pressured into anything.

This study is looking at combination therapy to manage blood pressure in the lungs — a serious complication of scleroderma.

Testing whether a new topical approach could reduce the frequency and severity of Raynaud's episodes. Home-based components available.

---

**Body:**

Hi [First name],

On February 28th (or 29th in a leap year), the rare disease community comes together for Rare Disease Day.

More than 300 million people worldwide live with a rare disease. Most of those diseases have no approved treatment. And for many patients, the journey from first symptom to correct diagnosis takes years — sometimes decades.

Research is how that changes. And knowing that research exists is the first step.

This month, we're getting the rare disease community ready for February with a deep dive into three conditions TrialScreen is highlighting this year.

---

**Featured: Rare disease spotlight**

**🔹 Scleroderma / Systemic Sclerosis**
Active research: skin fibrosis, lung involvement, quality of life.
Australian sites open. Partnership with Scleroderma Australia.
[See all scleroderma trials →]

**🔹 Cystic Fibrosis**
